name: Adaptive Facility Footprint Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Eight suites activated with modular renovation baseline → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through pre-approved modular components and vendor agreements → Strategic: Enables rapid expansion while trading off initial safety checks and calibration time.
options: Maintain eight dedicated suites with fixed capacity and milestone-driven expansion, deferring capital investment until data quality and safety thresholds are met.
Scale to twelve suites with phased renovations and staged hiring, expanding only after throughput and annotation targets are demonstrated.
Adopt modular, co-located micro-facilities connected via federated analytics and a data-as-a-service partnership to enable rapid scaling and shared governance.
review: Trade-off: Capacity acceleration vs safety and data quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with hospital information systems and long-term maintenance costs.

name: Sensor Suite Architecture Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Initiate dry-EEG/mattress-sensor deployment → Systemic: 25% faster data processing throughput through richer datasets and streamlined workflows → Strategic: Elevates data fidelity while increasing privacy risk and device management complexity.
options: Limit sensing to on-site PSG nights with standard channels to minimize data management burden and maintain manual scoring.
Deploy tiered sensing including dry-EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, and occasional infrared video; escalate to full PSG nights on flagged events with clinician oversight.
Deploy edge-computing enabled sensing with federated analytics across Bonn and partner sites, enabling continuous real-time triage and a data-sharing marketplace.
review: Trade-off: Granularity vs privacy and intrusiveness. Weakness: Options fail to address potential data gaps due to sensor non-compliance or device failures.

name: Ethical Recruitment and Retention Framework
consequences: Immediate: Enrollment targets defined → Systemic: 20% higher enrollment pace and 15% lower attrition through flexible pathways and partner engagement → Strategic: Broadens representativeness and accelerates data accrual while maintaining ethical standards.
options: Enforce strict pre-admission pathway with limited channels, lengthy screening, and fixed stay windows to optimize data integrity.
Broaden recruitment through regional clinics, offer flexible stay durations, adaptive inclusion criteria, and structured participant engagement to reduce dropout.
Establish continuous enrollment via dynamic consent and national patient-network partnerships, supported by a participant-centric outreach flywheel and potential data-access revenue-sharing.
review: Trade-off: Enrollment velocity vs sample representativeness. Weakness: Options fail to consider inclusion of underrepresented groups and accessibility barriers.

name: Governance and Data Stewardship Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Policy baseline for data governance established → Systemic: 25% faster data curation and inter-rater concordance through standardized pipelines → Strategic: Builds trust with participants and funders, enabling compliant data sharing while balancing privacy.
options: Maintain internal governance with fixed external advisory, local storage only, and de-identified data sharing under university DUAs.
Adopt EEG-BIDS pipelines, dual scoring QA, and deposits to recognized repositories; implement privacy-by-design for all platforms.
Pursue interoperable federated governance with privacy-preserving analytics and a data marketplace offering tiered access and revenue sharing across Bonn and partners.
review: Trade-off: Openness vs privacy and data ownership. Weakness: Options fail to consider regulatory shifts and data sovereignty across jurisdictions.

name: Funding and Risk Portfolio Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Core grants secured with contingency budgeting → Systemic: 25% faster decision cycles through milestone-based releases and risk-aware planning → Strategic: Enables sustainable growth and translational potential while preserving scientific independence.
options: Pursue core DFG and University funding, maintain contingency buffers, defer industry partnerships unless essential.
Diversify funding with defined industry partnerships and staged grant renewals, maintain reserves, and align milestones with sponsor expectations.

Establish a data-access partnership model with industry and healthcare providers, monetizing de-identified datasets while retaining publications rights, paired with a Phase II interventional arm to demonstrate translational impact.
review: Trade-off: Ambition vs financial risk. Weakness: Options fail to consider long-term sustainability beyond three years and external funding volatility.

name: Localized Modular Footprint Diffusion
consequences: Immediate: Deploy eight suites with modular baselines → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through plug-in units and standardized procurement → Strategic: Expand while calibrating safety, interoperability, and long-term maintenance; trade-offs: speed versus risk.
options: Maintain eight suites with fixed capacity and milestone-driven expansion, deferring capital investment until data quality and safety thresholds are met.
Scale to twelve suites with phased renovations and staged hiring, expanding only after throughput and annotation targets are demonstrated.
Adopt modular, co-located micro-facilities connected via federated analytics and a data-sharing consortium to enable rapid scaling and unified governance.
review: Trade-off: Capacity acceleration vs safety and data quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with hospital information systems and long-term maintenance costs.

name: Federated Sensing and Privacy-First Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Initiate tiered sensing with dry-EEG and mattress sensors → Systemic: 25% faster data curation through datasets and privacy-preserving processing → Strategic: Elevates data fidelity while increasing device management complexity and risk.
options: Limit sensing to on-site PSG nights with standard channels to minimize data management burden and maintain manual scoring.
Adopt tiered sensing including dry-EEG and mattress sensors; escalate to full PSG nights on flagged events with clinician oversight.
Implement edge-computing enabled sensing with federated analytics across Bonn and partner sites, enabling cross-site collaboration and risk-aware triage.
review: Trade-off: Granularity vs privacy and intrusiveness. Weakness: The options fail to address data gaps due to sensor non-compliance or device failures.

name: Dynamic Enrollment and Retention Engineering
consequences: Immediate: Expand enrollment pathways with flexible stays and dynamic screening → Systemic: 25% faster enrollment via consent optimization and partner networks → Strategic: Broader representativeness and faster data accrual with ethical safeguards.
options: Enforce strict pre-admission pathway with limited channels, lengthy screening, and fixed stay windows to optimize data integrity.
Broaden recruitment through regional clinics, offer flexible stay durations, adaptive inclusion criteria, and structured participant engagement to reduce dropout.
Implement dynamic consent with continuous engagement via digital channels and regional registries, enabling rapid multi-site enrollment while maintaining governance safeguards.
review: Trade-off: Enrollment velocity vs representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider inclusion of underrepresented groups and accessibility barriers.

name: Open Governance and Compliance Scaffold
consequences: Immediate: Establish baseline governance with oversight and local storage → Systemic: 25% faster data curation and inter-rater concordance via standardized pipelines → Strategic: Builds trust and enables data reuse while preserving privacy.
options: Establish baseline governance with oversight and local storage → Systemic: 25% faster data curation and inter-rater concordance via standardized pipelines → Strategic: Builds trust and enables data reuse while preserving privacy.
Adopt EEG-BIDS pipelines, dual scoring QA, and deposits to recognized repositories; implement privacy-by-design for all platforms.
Implement federated governance with privacy-preserving analytics and cross-institution data reuse under controlled access with a regional data consortium.
review: Trade-off: Openness vs privacy and data ownership. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory shifts and data sovereignty across jurisdictions.

name: Diversified Financing and Risk Allocation
consequences: Immediate: Secure core grants with contingencies and reserves → Systemic: 25% faster decision cycles via milestone-based releases and risk-aware buffers → Strategic: Enables sustainable growth and translational potential while preserving scientific independence.
options: Pursue core DFG and University funding, defer industry partnerships unless essential.
Diversify funding with defined industry partnerships and staged grant renewals, maintain reserves, align milestones with sponsor expectations.
Create a cross-sector data-sharing consortium with tiered access and sponsor-driven revenue sharing, while planning a Phase II interventional arm to demonstrate translational impact.
review: Trade-off: Ambition vs financial risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term sustainability beyond three years and external funding volatility.

name: Community-Driven Research Ecosystem Design
consequences: Immediate: Establish community advisory board and dynamic consent pilots → Systemic: 25% faster enrollment and retention through trusted regional partnerships → Strategic: Enhances representativeness and sustained engagement, but increases governance overhead and consent fatigue.
options: Formalize community advisory board with fixed consent windows, robust privacy controls, and predictable engagement cadence.
Scale regional partnerships with dynamic consent pilots and flexible participation, expanding inclusivity.
Launch participatory data commons powered by trusted execution environments and blended funding to co-design studies.
review: Trade-off: Engagement speed vs privacy/governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability with national ethics approvals and cross-border data flows.

name: Cross-Institutional Data Stewardship Compact
consequences: Immediate: Establish data-sharing terms with partner institutions → Systemic: 25% faster cross-site harmonization via common standards → Strategic: Expands collaboration and accelerates method validation, but increases legal/compliance overhead.
options: Limit data sharing to de-identified data within Bonn and current partner DUAs.
Define standardized data models and establish a lightweight cross-site collaboration framework with governance.
Create an inter-institutional data lake with TEEs-enabled cross-site analytics and blended funding for a researcher network.
review: Trade-off: Openness of data sharing vs compliance burden and data sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider data sovereignty and vendor lock-in risk.

name: Safety-First Longitudinal Participant Welfare Engine
consequences: Immediate: Implement proactive welfare monitoring and rapid escalation triggers → Systemic: 25% reduction in adverse events and attrition through proactive support → Strategic: Improves ethical standing and data quality, but increases operating costs and staffing complexity.
options: Enforce strict safety protocols with fixed staffing ratios and minimal variance.
Add welfare liaisons and dynamic escalation policies with flexible stay windows.
Implement real-time welfare optimization using TEEs-enabled analytics and non-invasive sensors to guide clinician escalation and privacy-preserving interventions.
review: Trade-off: Participant safety vs. operating cost and staffing. Weakness: The options fail to consider caregiver burnout and long-term staff retention.

name: Annotation Throughput and Validation Framework
consequences: Immediate: Standardize scoring with dual raters and templates → Systemic: 25% faster annotation throughput via streamlined pipelines and calibration → Strategic: Improves reliability and publication quality, but increases training burden and scorer fatigue.
options: Maintain current scoring with two independent raters and adjudication.
Adopt semi-automated annotation aids and structured scoring templates.
Deploy TEEs-enabled triage with on-device tagging and cross-site annotation network.
review: Trade-off: Automation vs scorer accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to anticipate calibration drift and scorer turnover over long durations.

name: Regulatory Agility and Risk Hedging Schema
consequences: Immediate: Map EU and German regulatory requirements → Systemic: 25% faster compliance reporting via standardized templates → Strategic: Reduces risk of delays and sanctions, but elevates administrative overhead and governance coordination.
options: Maintain local compliance approach and limit external sharing.
Develop centralized compliance playbooks with cross-border governance and joint audits.
Create a regional compliance sandbox with TEEs-backed cross-jurisdiction data exchange and automated governance workflows.
review: Trade-off: Regulatory agility vs governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential changes in funding landscapes and political risk.
